_comment: Hand-authored tip sections, prepended to the catalog's Final Notes in the order listed here. id must be unique across the merged tip list.
title: Make sure you love every dish
body:
**Hold onto your love of your craft.**
Every plate is more focused and built around a smaller set of ingredients — but take this as an opportunity to keep honing and improving your craft. Take on these new constraints as a challenge and creative fuel to become the best chef you can be.

**A kinder menu is a whole new world.**
Moving toward plant-based isn't subtraction. It's recipes, techniques, and experiments you'd never have touched otherwise — koji-cured roots, fermented bases, maitake "steaks," vegetable garums, charcoal-grilled celery hearts. The surface area is huge, and the plate gets more interesting, not less.

**Build dishes guests adore.**
Make your food ==fresh==, tasty, and unforgettable. Know what your guests are actually coming for. Light, easy-on-the-stomach plates? Fine dining for a romantic night out? Comfort food that reminds them of home? "Exquisite" looks completely different across those rooms — match the dish program to the audience and to the story you're trying to tell them.

Then service-test until the dish is beautiful, the texture sings, the seasoning is dialed in. Make plates your guests come back for — and tell their friends about.

title: Marketing plant-based dishes successfully
body:
**Lead with the food, not the label.**
*Slow-braised maitake bourguignon* (no cruelty) beats *vegan stew* or *vegan bourguignon*. Consider dropping "vegan" from dish names — unless you're in a vegan-friendly market like Portland, New York City, Los Angeles, or London — and use *no cruelty* or *kill-free* as the values tag instead.

Avoid lazy substitution words too. Naming everything *mushroom X*, *vegetable Y*, or *lentil Z* gets boring fast. Keep names fresh and curious: *seared maitake*, *charred celery hearts*, *koji-cured root*, *fermented chickpea curd*. Each dish name should make a guest want to taste.

**Frame impact in concrete numbers.**
Where you call out the values, swap "vegan" for the actual impact:
- Cruelty-free — no animals or furry friends harmed for this treat
- ~1,500 gallons of water saved per pound (vs. beef)
- 20× less land per calorie

A menu footer like *"every 10 plates here saves ~1,500 gallons of water and harms no animals"* lands harder than a 100% vegan sticker.

**Build the brand around craft.**
Press and word-of-mouth follow chefs who lead with technique. *"The maitake bourguignon at Gauthier"* travels further than "the vegan place on 5th."

title: Adding plant-based options to an existing menu
body:
**Don't separate the plant menu.**
Resist a "plant-based options" sidebar or a back-page "vegan menu." Be proud of your plant dishes and don't stop until you are — they belong on the same page as the rest of the menu, in the same sections, as everything else. A roped-off section signals "not for you."

**Lead with the strongest plant dishes.**
Anchor starters and mains with plates that don't need a meat counterpart — mole, mujadara, mushroom bourguignon, hummus. Bury the meat versions in a smaller "also available" section.

**Close the menu with the "why."**
A back-page block, three sentences max:
- Water saved per plate (gallons, not %).
- Land + emissions vs. the meat alternative.
- Cruelty-free — no animal harmed, ever.

Finish with one line about your sourcing. End: *"every time you choose plant-based here, you save water, land, and lives."*

**Combo plates and pairings.**
Bundle plant-based starters, sides, and desserts into combo deals — even with your non-plant mains. "Main + **falafel** starter + **mango pudding** $X." "Any entrée + plant-based add-on $2." Guests meet the plant menu inside familiar combos, the price psychology nudges them in, and the bulk-prep dishes you already make get more covers per shift.

**Train the floor.**
"Want to try our [signature plant dish]?" beats "we have vegan options." Servers decide whether the default lands or feels like a tax — script the upsell, taste-test the dishes with them so they recommend with conviction.

**Mark dishes clearly but subtly.**
Even with no separate section, guests still need to spot the plant plates at a glance — vegans scanning for their safe orders, curious omnivores noticing the marker and trying one. A small inline glyph next to the dish name does the job: a green leaf, a "v," a discreet symbol. Keep it icon-sized, not color-blocked, with a one-line legend at the bottom of the menu rather than a big key up top. Legibility, not segregation.

title: How to save the world
body:
**Encourage the plant-based choice.**
These small moves turn the menu into a quiet win for the planet and for all the wonderful creatures that inhabit it — make plant the default, anchor it with concrete impact, and reward the choice so guests feel like they're winning, not compromising.

**Make plant-based the default.**
List the plant version first; offer dairy/meat as the upcharge tier ("+$3 for chicken"). Most guests won't ask. The base ticket stays cheaper, your kitchen runs more bulk-prep dishes, and the menu stops reading as "compromise food."

**Anchor the choice with impact.**
Next to plant options, a tiny inline note: *"saves ~1,500 gal water · cruelty-free."* Concrete numbers move people; preachy lines don't.

**Reward the choice.**
Build a small loyalty program around plant-based orders — $1 off, a stamped card after 10 plant plates, a free side after 5. The plant choice should feel like winning, not compromising.

**Bundle into combos.**
Pair plant-based starters, sides, or desserts with mains — even with the meat plates on the menu. A guest who orders a steak and gets **hummus** with it, or finishes with a **brigadeiro**, has had two plant plates without "deciding" to. That's a third of their meal on the kinder side, and the door opens for next time.

title: Operate sustainably
body:
**Watch the cost.**
Store-bought plant proteins can blow past your price point fast. Two ways out: build in-house alternatives, or be deliberate with portion sizes. A spoon of Just Egg on a **bibimbap** is plenty — don't pour half a bottle. The branded SKU is a flavor highlight, not the whole dish.

**Make your central proteins in-house if you're a budget build.**
Seitan, tofu, tempeh, and lentils are the four pillars of low-cost protein. Each one runs a fraction of the branded faux-meat alternative — scratch seitan ~$0.60/lb vs. ~$7–9/lb for Beyond ground; homemade tempeh half the cost of supermarket bricks; a mung-bean "egg" mix is pennies vs. ~$7/bottle for Just Egg. The trade-off is prep labor — but labor is fixed cost you've already budgeted, while ingredient cost is the variable that actually moves margin.

**Learn the food science to save costs.**
Each pillar protein has dozens of preparation tricks that change texture, flavor absorption, and chew — and the chef who knows them turns cheap ingredients into showpieces.
- Freeze and thaw your tofu before cooking. The ice crystals open the structure so it soaks up marinade like a sponge ([Eater explainer](https://www.eater.com/24195818/freezer-tofu-hack-texture-change)).
- Cold-pressed tofu gives a meatier bite; silken tofu blended with cashews is your dairy-free cream sauce.
- Steamed seitan is tender; baked seitan is jerky-firm; seitan boiled in stock gets the dense, sliceable texture for sandwiches.
- Tempeh simmered in broth before pan-frying loses its bitterness and gains umami.
- Lentils bloomed in oil + tomato paste before simmering develop a meaty depth no quick boil can match.

The chefs who win on cost are the ones who treat plant proteins as a craft, not a substitute. Read, taste, experiment.

**Faux-meat rule.**
Lead with the brand when the dish *is* the meat — picanha, Big Mac, Wellington. Keep tofu or legume primary when the dish *is* the legume (**mapo tofu**, **falafel**, dal); offer the faux-meat as an upcharge tier instead of the default protein.

**Portion deliberately.**
Plant proteins are flavor highlights, not bulk fillers. Build the plate around legumes, grains, and vegetables; let the faux-meat (or premium plant protein) be the accent. A 2 oz portion of Chunk Steak in a **bibimbap** reads "premium" on the menu and costs you ~$1.40, not $5.60.

**Quote quarterly.**
Wholesale faux-meat pricing is volatile — Impossible and Beyond have cut foodservice prices ~15–20% twice since 2021. Re-quote every quarter; lock in the dip. The dishes built around the lowest-cost protein win the menu.

Don't worry — we expect alt-protein and plant-based product pricing will keep coming down as these companies grow. ==The trend is on your side.==
